digest.top_line: ROADS: surgical bed recurrence 1% vs 12% for intraop GammaTile brachy vs post-op cavity SRS in resected brain mets >2cm — challenges the SRS standard.
digest.tldr: RT drove the day's actionable news: ROADS (CNS) favors intraop brachytherapy over cavity SRS on local control, and Neo-CRAG (upper-GI) revives neoadjuvant chemoRT in gastric cancer (mOS 67.5 vs 37.6mo) where CRITICS/TOPGEAR were null. Prostate ran a genomic-classifier thread on an RT+ADT backbone.

sites.intro: MIBC bladder-preservation thread: durvalumab added to 55Gy/20fx chemoRT (RAD-IO) plus post-pCR perioperative management.

studies.name: RAD-IO
studies.tldr: 12-mo DFS 40/50 (80%, 95% CI 0.67-0.89) cleared the pre-set ≥75% GO bar for durvalumab added to 5FU/MMC chemoRT in MIBC.
details: 📊 Met the pre-set GO bar: framework GO ≥75%, contextual 60-75%, NO-GO <60%
🔍 Single-arm multi-stage feasibility/safety trial; T2-T4a N0M0 urothelial MIBC
📐 RT 55Gy/20fx to bladder; node-positive expansion added 46Gy/20fx to nodes
💊 Durvalumab 1500mg neoadjuvant + concurrent + adjuvant (to 12mo post-chemoRT)
💊 Concurrent 5-FU 500 mg/m²/24h ×5d + mitomycin C 12 mg/m²
🔍 Feasible/tolerable; AEs consistent with component chemoRT + durvalumab
🔗 Benchmark BC2001 (JCO 2016): CT vs no-CT DFS HR 0.78 (0.60-1.02), p=0.07 · the contextual comparator, not a RAD-IO result
⚠️ Single-arm; no randomised IO comparator, so 'higher OS/DFS vs prior trials' is benchmarked not head-to-head
⚠️ 12-mo DFS is an early surrogate; small N (~50 evaluable); node+ data only 6 pts

figures.caption: Durvalumab (N=54): completed planned treatment 33 (61%), discontinued early 21 (39%).

verdict.rationale: Single-arm feasibility stage, no randomised IO comparator; benchmarked against historical BC2001. 12-mo DFS an early surrogate at small N; met pre-set GO bar for further evaluation only.
verdict.audience: T2-T4a N0M0 muscle-invasive urothelial bladder cancer

studies.significance: RT is standard and transferable: 55Gy/20fx to bladder, plus 46Gy/20fx elective nodal coverage in the node-positive expansion, extending durvalumab-chemoRT preservation to N+ disease. 12-mo DFS 80% (40/50, 95% CI 0.67-0.89) cleared the pre-set ≥75% GO bar vs BC2001. Moves whether to layer IO onto a hypofractionated preservation backbone.
studies.monday_clinic: In T2-T4a N0M0 MIBC opting for bladder preservation, this supports durvalumab layered onto 5FU/MMC chemoRT as worth further evaluation; it does not yet establish that IO improves on chemoRT alone, and node-positive experience rests on only 6 expansion pts.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Single-arm**, multi-stage feasibility and safety trial testing durvalumab added to 5FU/MMC chemoradiation for bladder preservation. Benchmarked against **BC2001** historical CRT data, not a randomised comparator.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **T2-T4a N0M0** urothelial muscle-invasive bladder cancer, stratified by prior neoadjuvant chemotherapy (yes vs no). A stage-1 expansion added the **first 6 node-positive** pts.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: **Durvalumab 1500 mg** given neoadjuvant, synchronous, and adjuvant to **12 months** post-chemoRT. Concurrent **5-FU 500 mg/m²/24h ×5 days** and **mitomycin C 12 mg/m²**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **55 Gy in 20 fractions** to bladder (hypofractionated). Node-positive expansion added **46 Gy/20 fx** elective nodal RT.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** 12-month disease-free survival post-chemoRT, read against a GO/NO-GO framework (**GO ≥75%**, contextual 60-75%, NO-GO <60%).

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: 12-mo DFS **40/50 (80%)**, 95% CI 0.67-0.89, clearing the ≥75% GO bar. **33/54 (61%)** completed planned durvalumab; investigators reported very high bladder-preservation and DFS vs prior trial data.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: AEs reported **in line with component therapies** (chemoRT plus durvalumab). **21/54 (39%)** discontinued durvalumab early.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Benchmarked against **BC2001** (James, JCO 2016), where adding 5FU/MMC to RT gave DFS **HR 0.78 (0.60-1.02), p=0.07**. RAD-IO's 80% 12-mo DFS is claimed higher than prior data, but the comparison is **non-randomised**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents **T2-T4a N0M0 muscle-invasive urothelial bladder cancer** suitable for bladder-preservation chemoRT. Does not represent metastatic disease, radical-cystectomy-preferred pts, or node-positive disease beyond the 6-patient expansion.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Single-arm** with a historical benchmark and no concurrent IO comparator; 12-mo **DFS is an early surrogate**; small N (~50 evaluable); node-positive experience limited to 6 pts.

open_questions: Does durvalumab add over chemoRT alone in a randomised trial
Durability of bladder preservation and DFS beyond 12 months
Efficacy in node-positive MIBC beyond the 6-patient expansion

sites.intro: Genomic-classifier thread on a fixed RT+ADT/ARPI backbone: Decipher for intensification gating and docetaxel selection, mostly prognostic not predictive.

studies.name: Genomic Classifier + NCCN Risk Stratification (Abstract 5000)
studies.tldr: 22-gene GC independently prognostic (MFS/DM/OS, p<0.001); combined NCCN+GC score reclassifies ~¼ of high-risk pts to gate AAP intensification on an RT+ADT backbone.
details: 📊 22-gene GC (Decipher) independently improved prognostic estimates over clinical variables for MFS, DM, and OS (multivariable, p<0.001)
🔍 Population: NCCN ≥ high-risk localized prostate; RT+ADT is the fixed definitive backbone for all

details.text: 🔍 Clinico-transcriptomic (CT) score = NCCN clinical points + 22-gene GC points

details: 📊 Output rule: ≤2 pts = CT-HR → RT+ADT; ≥3 pts = CT-VHR → RT+ADT+AAP (abiraterone) intensification
🔗 ~¼ of NCCN ≥HR pts are clinical/GC-discordant, so GC moves the risk band (and the intensification call) in a meaningful fraction
💊 Intensification rationale is imported from STAMPEDE M0 (Attard, Lancet 2022) AAP benefit; MFS/OS HRs shown in figure
⚠️ Prognostic, not predictive: shows GC stratifies risk, not that CT-VHR pts derive MORE AAP benefit; no randomized biomarker×treatment interaction
⚠️ Retrospective GC validation on NRG cohort, externally benchmarked to STAMPEDE curves; not a prospective intensification RCT

verdict.rationale: Retrospective GC prognostic validation, not a randomized predictive-biomarker trial; consistent with prior Decipher/NRG data. Intensification benefit borrowed from STAMPEDE, not shown to be GC-predictive.

studies.significance: ~¼ of NCCN ≥HR pts are clinical/GC-discordant, so the 22-gene GC reclassifies who reaches CT-VHR (≥3 pts) and has AAP added onto the fixed RT+ADT backbone. The intensification it triggers carries STAMPEDE M0 magnitude (MFS HR 0.53, OS HR 0.60). It sharpens the intensification decision, not RT dose or target.
studies.monday_clinic: In NCCN high-risk/very-high-risk localized prostate planned for definitive RT+ADT, this supports GC-informed CT-VHR (≥3 pts) selection for AAP intensification; it does not extend to intermediate-/low-risk disease or change RT dose or target.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Retrospective** validation of the 22-gene GC (Decipher) layered on NCCN clinical risk in NCCN ≥ high-risk localized prostate (NRG cohort), externally benchmarked to STAMPEDE M0 curves (Attard, Lancet 2022). **Not a prospective RCT.**

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: NCCN **high-risk and very-high-risk** localized prostate cancer, all planned for **definitive RT+ADT**.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: **CT-HR (≤2 pts) → RT+ADT**; **CT-VHR (≥3 pts) → RT+ADT+AAP** (abiraterone acetate + prednisone) intensification.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: GC is **independently prognostic** for MFS, DM, and OS over clinical variables (multivariable, **p<0.001**). Roughly **one-quarter** of NCCN ≥HR pts are clinical/GC-discordant, reclassifying risk band and the intensification call.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: AAP intensification magnitude is **borrowed from STAMPEDE M0** (Attard, Lancet 2022), not generated here; GC prognostic value aligns with prior Decipher/NRG validation.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents NCCN high-risk and very-high-risk localized prostate cancer on definitive RT+ADT. Does not represent intermediate- or low-risk disease, node-positive or metastatic disease, or the radical-prostatectomy pathway.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Prognostic, not predictive**: no randomized biomarker×treatment interaction showing CT-VHR pts gain more from AAP. Intensification benefit imported from an external trial's average effect.

open_questions: Does GC-guided AAP intensification improve outcomes prospectively?
Is GC predictive of differential AAP benefit, not just prognostic?
Optimal AAP intensification threshold in clinical/GC-discordant pts

studies.name: A-DREAM
studies.tldr: 41% remained treatment-free with eugonadal testosterone recovery at 18mo after interrupting ADT+ARPI in deep-responding mHSPC (1° EP met, one-sided p=0.0249).
details: 🔍 Phase 2 single-arm (Alliance), N=78 mHSPC deep responders interrupt ADT+ARPI; median FU 26.9mo (enrolled 07/2022-03/2024)
🔍 Entry: PSA<0.2 (stable/falling) after 18-24mo ADT + ≥12mo ARPI; 64.9% CHAARTED low-volume, median age 70
🔍 Re-initiation triggered by PSA ≥5 ng/ml, PCWG3 radiographic progression, or cancer-related symptoms
📊 rPFS 15/78 events, median not reached (95% CI 32.5-NE)
📊 OS 4/78 events, median not reached (deaths: prostate cancer, MDS, influenza, MI)
⚠️ Primary gap is testosterone recovery, not progression: many stay treatment-free but recover T slowly (median 9.0mo) in this median-70yo cohort
⚠️ Single-arm, no randomised comparator vs continued therapy; survival cost of stopping is unquantified
🔗 Distinct from classic intermittent-ADT trials (SWOG 9346): interruption here follows a deep ADT+ARPI response, a more selected modern population

figures.caption: Median time to eugonadal T 9.0mo. T recovery by 18mo 52 (66.7%); treatment-free 18mo 45 (57.7%); primary endpoint (treatment-free + T recovery) 32 (41.0%), 80% CI 33.1-48.9%, one-sided p 0.0249

verdict.rationale: Single-arm phase 2, N=78, no randomised comparator vs continued therapy; primary endpoint met but interruption safety needs longer follow-up and randomisation.
verdict.audience: mHSPC deep responder (PSA<0.2) after 18-24mo ADT + ≥12mo ARPI

studies.monday_clinic: In metastatic HSPC deep responders (PSA<0.2 after ≥18mo ADT and ≥12mo ARPI, predominantly low-volume), this supports discussing a monitored ADT+ARPI holiday with scheduled PSA/imaging; it does not extend to incomplete responders or high-volume disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 2 single-arm** Alliance trial. **N=78** eligible metastatic HSPC deep responders **interrupt ADT+ARPI**. Enrolled 07/2022-03/2024; median follow-up 26.9mo.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Entry required **PSA<0.2** (stable/falling) after 18-24mo ADT and **≥12mo ARPI**. Median age 70 (49-90); **64.9% low-volume**, 35.1% high-volume (CHAARTED); 51.3% had prior local radiation, 29.5% metastasis-directed radiation.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** treatment-free with eugonadal testosterone (≥150 ng/dL) at 18 months. Exploratory: rPFS, TTNT, OS, cost.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Primary met: **32/78 (41.0%)** treatment-free and eugonadal at 18mo (80% CI 33.1-48.9%, one-sided p=0.0249). 57.7% were treatment-free at 18mo and 66.7% recovered testosterone; median time to eugonadal T **9.0mo**.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Disease control held through interruption: rPFS 15/78 events, OS 4/78 events, both medians not reached. The 41% primary is capped by **slow testosterone recovery, not progression**, so the endpoint understates short-term oncologic safety in this older cohort.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents metastatic HSPC deep responders (PSA<0.2 after ≥18mo ADT + ≥12mo ARPI), low-volume-predominant. Does not represent incomplete responders, high-volume disease, or patients unwilling to accept delayed testosterone recovery.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Single-arm with **no randomised comparator** against continued therapy, so the survival cost of interruption is unquantified. Median follow-up 26.9mo is short for mHSPC; durability of treatment-free intervals is unproven.

open_questions: Survival cost of interruption vs continued ADT+ARPI
Durability of treatment-free interval beyond 27 months
Which deep responders can safely interrupt

studies.name: ARACOG (AFT-47)
studies.tldr: MCCD -36.1 (enza) vs -15.8 (daro), P=0.009 at 24 wk: enzalutamide worse for cognition; randomized open-label phase 2, N=111 advanced prostate.
details: 📊 1° EP met: greater cognitive decline on enzalutamide vs darolutamide at 24 wk (MCCD median change -36.1 vs -15.8, P=0.009)
🔍 Randomized open-label phase 2, N=111 (mHSPC, mCRPC, nmCRPC); DAR vs ENZ, stratified by age (<65, 65-80, >80)
🔍 1° EP = between-arm % change baseline to 24 wk in Maximally Changed Cognitive Domain (MCCD), from 5 CANTAB tests
🔍 Primary analysis N=95 (daro 48, enza 47); crossovers scored at time of crossover in their randomized arm
⚠️ Open-label: pts knew assignment; enza provided via standard of care (daro by study) may have skewed crossover
⚠️ CANTAB modules are research tools, not clinical diagnostics; MCCD picks each arm's worst domain (PALFAM daro, SWM enza)
⚠️ US academic centers only, predominantly White; most non-White pts randomized to enzalutamide (race-by-arm confound)
🔗 Direction fits darolutamide's low blood-brain-barrier penetration vs enzalutamide's CNS exposure (fatigue/cognitive AEs in prior enza trials)

verdict.rationale: Randomized head-to-head met its prespecified cognitive endpoint; aligns with darolutamide's known low CNS penetration. Phase 2, open-label, small N keep it below practice-changing.
verdict.audience: Advanced prostate cancer (mHSPC/mCRPC/nmCRPC) starting an ARSI

studies.monday_clinic: In advanced prostate cancer pts where cognitive tolerability drives ARSI selection, this randomized head-to-head supports darolutamide over enzalutamide for cognitive sparing; it does not address efficacy or compare against apalutamide or abiraterone.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomized **open-label phase 2**, N=111, darolutamide vs enzalutamide, stratified by age (<65, 65-80, >80). US academic centers only; enrolled 8/2021 to 3/2025, crossover permitted.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Advanced prostate cancer across **mHSPC, mCRPC, and nmCRPC**. Required acceptable enzalutamide co-pay; predominantly White, with most non-White pts randomized to enzalutamide.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: **Darolutamide provided by the study** vs **enzalutamide through standard of care**, an asymmetry that could bias crossover and adherence.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** between-arm % change from baseline to 24 wk in Maximally Changed Cognitive Domain (MCCD), from 5 remotely delivered CANTAB tests. Crossovers scored at crossover in their randomized arm.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: MCCD median change **-36.1 (enza, N=47) vs -15.8 (daro, N=48), P=0.009**: greater cognitive decline on enzalutamide.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: First randomized head-to-head of cognition for these two ARSIs; direction fits darolutamide's minimal blood-brain-barrier penetration vs enzalutamide's CNS exposure.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents advanced prostate cancer pts (mHSPC, mCRPC, nmCRPC) choosing between darolutamide and enzalutamide. Does not represent apalutamide, abiraterone, or head-to-head efficacy comparisons.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations

studies.name: Precision Oncology: Applying New Biomarkers in GU Cancers
studies.tldr: Biomarker round-up: tissue HRR/tumor-suppressor testing is SOC, but genomic classifiers show 24% clinical-risk discordance and lack ARPI-era validation.
details: 🔬 ASCO26 education session (J. Lang): a biomarker is useful only if it changes management; GU therapy selection stays biomarker-limited
📊 Tissue-panel testing for HRR + tumor-suppressor loss is called SOC in prostate
📊 Landscape-slide annotations, approx. eligible fraction: Lu-177 PSMA ~90%, PARP inhibitors ~10-20%, pembrolizumab ~3%
📊 Decipher genomic classifier discordant with clinical risk in 24% of high-risk events
⚠️ Discordance from tissue-available subset (n=427) of n=3816; representativeness of full trial population uncertain
⚠️ Source classifier trials predate ARPIs; applicability to current ARPI-based SOC questioned
🔍 ENZAMET biomarker cohort: 1,071 consented, GEP N=764, final Decipher cohort N=634 (56% of enrolled)
📊 Decipher DPMC ≤0.85 subgroup: ADT+enza+docetaxel had worse OS than ADT+enza (triplet cohort N=320)
⚠️ Confounded: docetaxel not randomized, with more high-volume disease + older pts → selection bias, not a validated predictor

figures.caption: Biomarker vs clinical-risk concordance in high-risk prostate: 24% discordant (tissue n=427 of 3816).

studies.significance: For RT readers using genomic classifiers to gate ADT intensification with salvage or definitive RT, the session's caution transfers: Decipher was discordant with clinical risk in 24% of high-risk pts (tissue available in only 427 of 3816), and its validation cohorts predate ARPIs. Treat the score as hypothesis-generating, not a settled gate.

studies.name: ENZAMET + Decipher (Part 2)
studies.tldr: Decipher >0.85 predicts docetaxel OS benefit on an ADT+enza backbone (HR 0.75 vs 1.94, interaction p=0.04); ≤0.85 can skip docetaxel.
details: 🔍 Decipher genomic classifier (DPMC) applied to ENZAMET's ADT + enzalutamide backbone cohort, N=320
🔍 Genomically high-risk defined as DPMC >0.85
🔍 Early docetaxel given at investigator discretion, not a randomised comparison
⚠️ Docetaxel effect estimated by IPTW propensity weighting, with residual imbalance acknowledged after scoring
⚠️ High-Decipher triplet benefit not independently significant (CI crosses 1), resting on the docetaxel-by-Decipher interaction (p=0.04)
⚠️ >0.85 benefit sharpened only after weighting (unweighted per-stratum HR near null), so treat as hypothesis-generating
🔗 Direction consistent with docetaxel OS benefit in CHARTED and STAMPEDE for high-volume/high-risk mHSPC
🔍 Low-volume subset showed the same direction, but small N and wide CIs (exploratory)

verdict.rationale: Post-hoc biomarker-by-docetaxel interaction; docetaxel not randomised (IPTW-weighted, residual imbalance acknowledged). High-Decipher benefit CI crosses 1, rests on interaction p=0.04, strengthened only after weighting.
verdict.audience: mHSPC on ADT + enzalutamide, Decipher genomic classifier available

studies.monday_clinic: In mHSPC starting ADT + enzalutamide, a Decipher score >0.85 is where the docetaxel OS benefit concentrates while ≤0.85 sees little added benefit; the read informs docetaxel selection, not any radiotherapy decision.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Preplanned biomarker analysis of the phase 3 ENZAMET RCT. Decipher genomic classifier (DPMC, cutpoint **>0.85**) applied to the ADT + enzalutamide backbone cohort, **N=320**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **Metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer** on an ADT + enzalutamide backbone with an available Decipher score. A low-volume subset was analysed separately.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: ADT + enzalutamide ± early **docetaxel**. Docetaxel was given at investigator discretion (a stratification factor), so the docetaxel comparison is **not randomised**.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Two-part signal: Decipher >0.85 is prognostic for worse OS on ADT+enza, and by IPTW interaction (**p=0.04**) predicts OS benefit from added docetaxel; **≤0.85** derives little benefit. Effect sizes in the figures.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Consistent with docetaxel OS benefit in **CHARTED** and **STAMPEDE** for high-volume/high-risk mHSPC; positions Decipher as a molecular refinement of that selection. Presented as **Level 1B** evidence.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents mHSPC pts on an ADT + enzalutamide backbone with a Decipher score. Does not represent pts managed without a Decipher score, or on systemic backbones other than ADT + enzalutamide.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Docetaxel not randomised (IPTW-weighted, residual imbalance after scoring); the high-Decipher benefit's CI crosses 1 and rests on a subgroup interaction. Hypothesis-generating, not yet practice-defining.

open_questions: Prospective validation of Decipher-guided docetaxel selection in mHSPC
Whether DPMC ≤0.85 pts can safely omit docetaxel
Decipher predictive value across other intensification agents (ARSI, PARP)

studies.name: TALAPRO-3
studies.tldr: 3yr rPFS 77% vs 56%, HR 0.48 (0.36-0.65), adding talazoparib to enzalutamide/ADT in HRR-deficient metastatic prostate cancer.
details: 📊 3yr rPFS 77% (67-85) vs 56% (50-62), ITT (both arms HRR-deficient)
🔍 Randomized, placebo-controlled: talazoparib vs placebo, each on enzalutamide/ADT (300 vs 299)
🔍 HRR-deficient metastatic prostate cancer; BRCA and non-BRCA HRR strata prespecified
💊 Talazoparib (PARP inhibitor) layered onto the ARSI backbone; tests PARP+ARSI in HRR-mutated disease
⚠️ Effect concentrated in BRCA; non-BRCA benefit more modest (subgroup HRs in figure)
⚠️ 1° EP is rPFS (surrogate); OS not reported in source, so survival + durability unproven
🔗 Extends the PARP+ARSI combination (TALAPRO-2, PROpel, MAGNITUDE) to the enzalutamide/ADT-backbone metastatic setting

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase 3, rPFS primary hit (HR 0.48); but rPFS is a surrogate and OS not reported. Aligns with PARP+ARSI direction (TALAPRO-2, PROpel, MAGNITUDE).

studies.monday_clinic: In HRR-deficient metastatic prostate cancer, this supports intensifying enzalutamide/ADT with talazoparib, benefit steepest in the BRCA subgroup; it does not extend to HRR-intact disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomized, placebo-controlled trial: **talazoparib+enzalutamide vs placebo+enzalutamide**, 300 vs 299 pts. Published NEJM May 30, 2026.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **HRR-deficient metastatic prostate cancer**, enzalutamide/ADT backbone both arms. Prespecified BRCA (104/103) and non-BRCA HRR (196/196) strata.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: Talazoparib (PARP inhibitor) added to enzalutamide plus ADT; control substituted placebo for talazoparib on the same ARSI backbone.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** imaging-based (radiographic) PFS. OS and other secondaries not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **ITT 3yr rPFS 77% vs 56%**, P<0.001; per-population HRs and medians in the figure. Benefit concentrated in the **BRCA** subgroup.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Extends PARP+ARSI combinations (TALAPRO-2, PROpel, MAGNITUDE) that established the class in HRR-altered mCRPC toward the enzalutamide/ADT-backbone metastatic setting.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents HRR-deficient metastatic prostate cancer on an enzalutamide/ADT backbone, BRCA-enriched. Does not represent HRR-intact disease or PARP-inhibitor use outside HRR alterations.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Primary endpoint is rPFS, a **surrogate**; OS not reported in source. Non-BRCA benefit is modest, so the HRR-wide result leans on BRCA. Talazoparib medians not reached, so follow-up is immature.

open_questions: Overall survival benefit, given rPFS is the surrogate primary endpoint

sites.intro: Pre- vs sub-pectoral implant reconstruction: modest patient-reported chest well-being gain against more unplanned implant loss.

studies.name: PREPEC
studies.tldr: Pre-pectoral implant improved 2yr chest well-being +4.8 pts (BREAST-Q, p=0.01) vs sub-pectoral, but more unplanned implant loss (21% vs 15%).
details: 🔍 International randomized phase 3 (OPBC-02): pre- vs sub-pectoral implant after skin- or nipple-sparing mastectomy
🔍 Eligible in both therapeutic and risk-reduction mastectomy settings; primary QoL analysis N=191 vs 189
⚠️ 1° endpoint is patient-reported (BREAST-Q chest); open-label surgical trial, so PRO awareness bias is possible
⚠️ 4.8-pt gain is statistically significant but modest on the 0-100 BREAST-Q scale
⚠️ Main secondary safety endpoint failed non-inferiority: more unplanned implant loss with pre-pectoral
📐 Implant-loss adjusted difference favored sub-pectoral but its 95% CI crossed zero (not statistically significant)
🔍 PRO completion 83-95% across 6 post-randomization timepoints; multiple-imputation, linear mixed-model analysis
📊 Net trade-off: better patient-reported chest well-being with pre-pectoral vs fewer implant losses with sub-pectoral

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase 3, primary endpoint met, but a modest patient-reported surrogate and an unmet non-inferiority safety endpoint keep it short of practice-changing.

studies.monday_clinic: In pts undergoing skin- or nipple-sparing mastectomy (therapeutic or risk-reducing) who choose implant reconstruction, this supports pre-pectoral placement for better 2yr patient-reported chest well-being, weighed against numerically higher unplanned implant loss (21% vs 15%).

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: International **randomized phase 3** trial (OPBC-02): **pre- vs sub-pectoral** implant reconstruction after skin- or nipple-sparing mastectomy. Open-label; **patient-reported primary endpoint** analyzed by multiple imputation and linear mixed models.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Women undergoing **skin- or nipple-sparing mastectomy** for cancer treatment or **risk reduction**, opting for implant reconstruction. Primary QoL analysis **N=191 pre- vs 189 sub-pectoral**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** long-term patient-reported physical well-being (chest), BREAST-Q, at **24 months**. **Main secondary safety:** unplanned loss or replacement of expander/implant.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: BREAST-Q chest well-being **79.2 vs 74.3** at 24mo, difference **4.8** (95% CI 1.0-8.7, **p=0.01**), favoring pre-pectoral. Longitudinal completion 83-95%.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Point estimate showed **more implant loss with pre-pectoral** (21.1% vs 14.5%), failing the trial's **non-inferiority** safety hypothesis; the adjusted-difference 95% CI (-2.4 to 13.8) crosses zero.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents women choosing implant-based reconstruction after skin- or nipple-sparing mastectomy, therapeutic or risk-reducing. Does not represent autologous (flap) reconstruction or non-implant approaches.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Open-label with a **patient-reported primary endpoint**, so awareness of implant type could bias PROs. The **4.8-point** gain is significant but modest, and the safety signal trended against pre-pectoral.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: A major RCT in an area of genuine equipoise. It supports pre-pectoral for **patient-reported chest well-being** while flagging a **higher implant-loss** signal, so the choice reads as a real **trade-off**, not a clean win.

open_questions: Durability of the QoL benefit and implant-loss gap beyond 24 months
Do outcomes differ in patients receiving post-mastectomy radiation?
Which patients accept higher implant-loss risk for better chest well-being?

sites.intro: Intraoperative brachytherapy challenges post-op cavity SRS for large resected brain mets, but watch the outsized OS gap for arm imbalance.

studies.name: ROADS
studies.tldr: Surgical bed recurrence 1% vs 12% and 2yr OS 62% vs 36% favoring intraoperative GammaTile brachytherapy over post-op SRS for resected brain mets >2cm.
details: 🔍 Randomized, N=230, resected brain met >2cm: intraoperative GammaTile (Cs-131 brachytherapy) placed at resection vs post-op SRS.
📊 Surgical bed recurrence 1% GammaTile vs 12% SRS.
🔍 >2cm cavities selected: exactly where single-fraction post-op SRS local control is weakest, the rationale for brachytherapy.
⚠️ 2yr OS gap (62% vs 36%) is large for a local-control intervention; a cavity-control effect is unlikely to move OS this much, watch for baseline arm imbalance.
⚠️ Leptomeningeal disease higher with brachytherapy: 10% GammaTile vs 3% SRS.
📐 Radiation necrosis comparable: 8% GammaTile vs 7% SRS.
🔍 Open-label (intraop brachy vs post-op SRS can't be blinded).
⚠️ OS was not a stated primary endpoint; abstract-only, not yet peer-reviewed.
🔗 Post-op cavity SRS is current standard for resected brain mets (N107C, Mahajan); ROADS challenges it, favoring intraop brachy on local control.

verdict.rationale: Randomized, primary endpoint (surg bed control) hit favoring intraop brachy over the post-op SRS standard; but abstract-only, open-label, and the large OS gain warrants scrutiny.
verdict.audience: Resected brain metastasis > 2 cm, post-op cavity RT candidates

studies.significance: The safety tradeoff the headline buries: leptomeningeal disease ran 10% with GammaTile vs 3% with SRS, even as surgical-bed recurrence fell to 1% vs 12%. Radiation necrosis was comparable (8% vs 7%). This moves the intraoperative-brachytherapy-vs-staged-cavity-SRS decision for resected mets >2 cm, where SRS local control is weakest.
studies.monday_clinic: In pts with a resected brain metastasis >2 cm needing cavity radiotherapy, this supports intraoperative brachytherapy as an alternative to post-op SRS for local control; it does not extend to intact (unresected) mets or cavities ≤2 cm.

primary_endpoint.name: Time to surgical bed recurrence
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: Surg bed recurrence 1% GammaTile vs 12% SRS

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomized trial, **N=230**, resected brain metastasis > 2 cm. GammaTile (Cs-131 collagen-tile brachytherapy) placed in the cavity at resection vs post-op stereotactic radiosurgery. ASCO 2026 final results. **Primary:** time to surgical bed recurrence and surgical-bed recurrence-free survival.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Resected brain metastasis **> 2 cm**, the larger-cavity setting where single-fraction post-op SRS local control is weakest. Histology / primary tumor not specified in source.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Experimental: intraoperative permanent Cs-131 seed brachytherapy (GammaTile) at resection. Control: post-op cavity SRS. Dose, fractionation, and cavity margin not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Both primary endpoints and 2yr OS favored GammaTile. Safety: **radiation necrosis comparable (8% vs 7%)**, leptomeningeal disease **higher with brachytherapy (10% vs 3%)**.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Post-op cavity SRS is the current standard for resected brain mets (N107C, Mahajan). ROADS **challenges it**, favoring intraoperative brachytherapy on local control.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents pts with a resected brain metastasis > 2 cm who are candidates for post-op cavity radiotherapy. Does not represent intact (unresected) brain mets or resection cavities ≤ 2 cm.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Abstract-only, not yet peer-reviewed; open-label. The 2yr OS advantage is large for a local therapy and **OS was not a stated primary endpoint**, raising the question of baseline arm imbalance.

open_questions: Whether the large OS gain reflects arm imbalance vs true benefit
Leptomeningeal failure risk with intraoperative brachytherapy
Applicability to resection cavities ≤ 2 cm

studies.name: CHRYSALIS-2
studies.tldr: mOS 41.0 mo (95% CI 27.7-NE) with 1L amivantamab + lazertinib in treatment-naïve atypical EGFR-mutant NSCLC; single-arm, n=49.
details: 📊 mOS 41.0 mo (95% CI 27.7-NE), ~3.5 years
🔍 single-arm cohort, n=49, 1L atypical EGFR-mutant advanced NSCLC; median f/u 31.3 mo
💊 median treatment duration 13.3 mo (range <0.1-53.2)
📊 39% remained on treatment >2 years
📊 OS independent of EGFR variant subtype (no clear association reported)
💊 safety consistent with prior reports, no new signals with longer f/u
🔗 extends 1L ami+laz durable-OS signal from common EGFR (exon19del/L858R) to atypical variants
⚠️ single-arm, no randomised comparator; can't establish SOC from these data
⚠️ atypical EGFR is a heterogeneous class; per-variant efficacy not detailed in source

figures.caption: Median OS 41.0 mo (95% CI 27.7-NE). Median follow-up 31.3 mo.
Time on treatment: median 13.3 mo (range <0.1-53.2); 39% on treatment >2 years.

verdict.rationale: Single-arm cohort (n=49), no randomised comparator in atypical EGFR NSCLC; durable but single-arm design caps the read at early-signal despite 31-mo follow-up.

studies.monday_clinic: In treatment-naïve atypical EGFR-mutant advanced NSCLC, a population with few standard targeted options, this single-arm 41-mo median OS supports amivantamab+lazertinib as a candidate 1L regimen; it does not extend to common EGFR (exon19del/L858R) disease, where randomised data already guide practice.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Single-arm** cohort, **n=49**, no randomised comparator. Median follow-up **31.3 mo**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Treatment-naïve (**1L**) atypical EGFR-mutated advanced NSCLC. Responses durable regardless of demographics, baseline mutations, and disease characteristics.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: IV **amivantamab + lazertinib**. Median treatment duration **13.3 mo** (range <0.1-53.2); **39%** on treatment >2 years.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Median OS **41.0 mo** (95% CI 27.7-NE), ~3.5 years. **No clear association** between EGFR variant subtype and OS.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: With longer follow-up, safety **consistent with prior reports**; no new safety signals.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Extends the durable 1L amivantamab+lazertinib OS signal from **common EGFR** (exon19del/L858R) to **atypical** variants.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents treatment-naïve atypical EGFR-mutant advanced NSCLC. Does not represent common EGFR (exon19del/L858R) or pretreated disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Single-arm**, n=49, no randomised comparator. Atypical EGFR is a **heterogeneous class**; per-variant efficacy not detailed in source.

open_questions: Efficacy by specific atypical EGFR variant
Randomised comparison vs other 1L options in atypical EGFR

studies.name: ESAONA
studies.tldr: iORR 95.5% vs 79.6% (p=0.0004) and icPFS HR 0.46 favoring asandeutertinib over osimertinib in 1L EGFR-mut NSCLC brain mets.
details: 🔍 Randomised phase 2, N=224 (111 vs 113), 1L EGFR-mutant NSCLC with brain metastases
🔍 All efficacy endpoints BICR-assessed (blinded independent central review)
⚠️ Time-to-event data immature: icPFS and overall PFS medians not reached in the asandeutertinib arm
⚠️ Overall PFS benefit borderline: HR 0.64, p=0.0473 (just under 0.05); intracranial signal far stronger

subdetails: Serious TRAEs: 10.8% vs 7.1% (numerically higher with asandeutertinib)

details: 🔗 Osimertinib is the current CNS-active 1L EGFR standard (FLAURA); asandeutertinib is positioned to raise the intracranial bar
⚠️ No OS reported in source; CNS endpoints are surrogate and follow-up short (medians not reached)

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase 2; strong intracranial ORR but time-to-event immature (medians NR), overall PFS borderline (p=0.0473), no OS. Needs phase 3 before displacing osimertinib.
verdict.audience: Treatment-naive EGFR-mutant NSCLC with brain metastases

studies.significance: The RT-relevant read is upfront local therapy: iORR 95.5% vs 79.6% and intracranial PFS not reached (HR 0.46) strengthen deferring upfront SRS/WBRT in favor of TKI in EGFR-mut brain mets. No head-to-head vs radiosurgery, so it moves sequencing, not RT omission at progression.
studies.monday_clinic: In treatment-naive EGFR-mutant NSCLC with brain metastases, this supports a TKI-first, SRS-deferred read; it does not extend to EGFR-wildtype disease or brain mets progressing on a prior EGFR TKI.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Randomised phase 2**, N=224 (**111 asandeutertinib vs 113 osimertinib**), 1L EGFR-mutant NSCLC with brain metastases. All efficacy endpoints **BICR-assessed**. Blinding not specified in source.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Treatment-naive (**1L**) **EGFR-mutant NSCLC with brain metastases**. Mutation subtype, symptomatic status, and prior brain RT not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: **Asandeutertinib** (next-generation EGFR TKI) vs **osimertinib**. Dose and schedule not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Intracranial endpoints (response, icPFS) drove the result and cleared significance more decisively than the **borderline overall PFS** benefit. Full arm comparison in the table above.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Any TRAEs **99.1% vs 95.6%**; serious TRAEs numerically higher with asandeutertinib (**10.8% vs 7.1%**). Fatal and discontinuation rates not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Osimertinib is the established **CNS-active 1L EGFR TKI** (FLAURA). Asandeutertinib raises the intracranial bar in phase 2, but no OS or phase 3 head-to-head yet.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents treatment-naive EGFR-mutant NSCLC with brain metastases. Does not represent EGFR-wildtype disease or brain metastases progressing on a prior EGFR TKI.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Phase 2 with **immature time-to-event data** (medians not reached); overall PFS benefit borderline (**p=0.0473**); **no OS**; blinding not specified.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Strong intracranial activity revives whether a CNS-active TKI can **defer upfront brain-directed RT/SRS** in EGFR-mutant NSCLC. **Phase 3 confirmation vs osimertinib**, plus OS and durability, is needed before displacing the standard.

open_questions: Overall survival vs osimertinib
Durability of intracranial response with longer follow-up
Head-to-head efficacy vs upfront radiosurgery

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3, open-label, multicenter RCT (NCT06448312); 1:1, **N=413**. Primary **PFS by BICR** crossed its interim efficacy boundary at 194 events; median follow-up **10.5 mo**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: 1L stage **IIIB/IIIC or IV** NSCLC, **PD-L1 TPS ≥1%** (22C3), **EGFR/ALK wild-type**, ECOG 0-1. Stratified by histology, PD-L1 (1-49 vs ≥50), ECOG.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **ORR 70.2% vs 42.0%**. PFS gain held across PD-L1 strata, strongest in **TPS 1-49%** where pembro monotherapy is weakest. OS immature, formal testing pending.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Current 1L PD-L1+ NSCLC standard is **pembrolizumab ± chemotherapy**. This tests a **chemo-free ADC+IO** doublet; no head-to-head vs chemo-IO.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Open-label**; OS immature at 10.5-mo follow-up and formally untested; PFS reported at an interim efficacy boundary. No safety data in source.

details: 🗞️ Rini ASCO26 GU discussant: can curative-intent GU therapy raise cure rates while preserving organ function and QoL?
🔍 Bladder preservation thread: durvalumab added to mitomycin C / 5-FU chemoRT (55 Gy/20 fx) in MIBC
💊 Durvalumab drove the early discontinuations (toxicity / PD); chemoRT delivery itself was not compromised

details.text: 🔍 EVP (enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab) bladder-sparing trials in MIBC

details: 🔗 RAMPART (adjuvant RCC): non-clear-cell subgroups have few pts/events and wide CIs; no proven adjuvant IO benefit in non-clear-cell RCC
📊 Adjuvant pembro (Choueiri, ASCO26): ctDNA stratifies DFS better than pathologic features; ctDNA-positive pts fare far worse in both arms

Supports adding IO to bladder-preservation chemoRT, but bladder-intact and QoL endpoints remain immature.

sites.intro: Neoadjuvant chemoRT is RT-attributable positive in high-risk gastric cancer, reopening a question CRITICS and TOPGEAR left null (though on a XELOX, not FLOT, backbone).

studies.name: Neo-CRAG
studies.tldr: Adding neoadjuvant chemoRT (45Gy/25fx) to periop XELOX improved mDFS 52.7 vs 24.4mo (HR 0.75) and mOS 67.5 vs 37.6mo in high-risk LAGC.
details: 📐 RT: 45 Gy/25 fx concurrent, started after chemo cycle 1, with dose-reduced XELOX (oxaliplatin 100, capecitabine 825) during RT
🔍 RT is the sole added variable: both arms get identical periop XELOX, and the CRT arm gets less chemo during RT, so the DFS/OS gain is RT-attributable
📊 Pathologic and locoregional outcomes (CRT vs CT)
🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=620 (310/arm), 13 China centers, 2013-2022; 36.3% EGJ (Siewert II/III); all planned standardized D2 gastrectomy
🔗 vs CRITICS (2018) / TOPGEAR (2024): adding RT to a chemo backbone was negative in both; a positive Neo-CRAG keeps the neoadjuvant chemoRT question contested
⚠️ Backbone is XELOX, not periop FLOT (current SOC); incremental value of RT on a FLOT backbone is untested
⚠️ G3+ hematologic toxicity higher with CRT: 14.6% (44/302) vs 10.3% (31/301)
📊 G3+ postoperative complication rates comparable: 9.0% (20/223) vs 7.6% (17/225)

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase 3, primary DFS and OS both hit, but positive chemoRT diverges from negative CRITICS/TOPGEAR; non-FLOT XELOX backbone limits transfer to current SOC.
verdict.audience: High-risk locally advanced gastric/EGJ (cT3N2-3 to cT4), fit for D2 resection

studies.significance: The RT read is clean attribution: both arms share the XELOX backbone and the CRT arm got dose-reduced chemo during RT, yet locoregional recurrence halved (9.4% vs 18.3%) with more ypN0 and downstaging. On 45Gy/25fx, this revives neoadjuvant chemoRT for high-risk D2-resected gastric/EGJ, on a non-FLOT backbone.
studies.monday_clinic: In high-risk cT3N2-3/cT4 gastric or EGJ adenocarcinoma planned for D2 gastrectomy on a XELOX backbone, these data support a DFS/OS and locoregional-control benefit from adding neoadjuvant chemoRT; the signal does not extend to FLOT-backbone or metastatic patients.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 open-label RCT, 1:1, **N=620** (310/arm), 13 Chinese referral centers, enrolled 2013-2022. **Primary:** disease-free survival; secondary OS, pCR, R0, safety.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: High-risk locally advanced gastric/EGJ adenocarcinoma: **cT3N2-3M0, cT4aN+M0, or cT4bNanyM0**; 36.3% EGJ (Siewert II/III). All planned standardized D2 gastrectomy.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: Both arms: 3 cycles preoperative + 3 cycles adjuvant XELOX (oxaliplatin 130, capecitabine 1000 BID D1-14, Q3W) around D2 gastrectomy.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: CRT arm only: concurrent **45 Gy/25 fx** begun after chemo cycle 1, with **dose-reduced** XELOX (oxaliplatin 100, capecitabine 825) during RT. RT is the sole added variable between arms.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Primary DFS met and secondary OS positive, both favoring CRT; pathologic downstaging, ypN0, and locoregional control also improved (see figure and table).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Contrasts with **CRITICS** and **TOPGEAR**, where adding radiotherapy to a chemo backbone did not improve survival. A positive DFS/OS here reopens neoadjuvant chemoRT in high-risk, D2-resected LAGC.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk locally advanced gastric/EGJ (Siewert II/III) adenocarcinoma, cT3N2-3 to cT4b, fit for D2 gastrectomy on a XELOX backbone. Does not represent FLOT-backbone, metastatic, or Western populations with lower D2-resection rates.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Open-label; **non-FLOT** XELOX backbone limits transfer to current perioperative standard. Single-country (China) with high D2-resection quality; DFS (not OS) was the primary endpoint.

open_questions: Incremental benefit of RT on a FLOT chemo backbone
Generalizability beyond high-quality-D2 Asian centers
Whether EGJ and distal gastric benefit equally

sites.intro: Post-hoc selection score for celiac-plexus SRS in pancreatic pain; awaits external validation.

studies.name: SPIN Score (Celiac Plexus SRS)
studies.tldr: 89% pain response at SPIN 2 vs 32% at SPIN 0 for celiac SRS; post-hoc selection score, n=90.
details: 🔍 Post-hoc analysis of 90 evaluable pts from the pivotal celiac SRS phase 2 (NCT03323489, Lancet Oncol 2024)
📊 Pivotal-trial pain response 53% (95% CI 42-64%); no actionable selection tool existed before this
🔍 SPIN (Severe Pain + Intact Nerves) score, 0-2: baseline pain >6 (+1), no prior neurotoxic chemo (+1)
📐 Score discrimination modest: AUC 0.716 apparent, 0.714 optimism-corrected (500-iteration bootstrap)
🔍 Target celiac plexus (D12-L2); SRS dose/fractionation not reported in source

subdetails: Post-hoc analysis of a single-arm parent trial; predictors associative, not causal
Internal bootstrap validation only; external validation pending before clinical use
Subjective BPI-SF pain endpoint at 3 weeks

verdict.rationale: Post-hoc predictor modeling of a single-arm phase 2; internal bootstrap validation only, external validation pending. Associations, not a prospectively validated selection tool.
verdict.audience: Pancreatic cancer, intractable retroperitoneal pain, celiac SRS candidates

studies.significance: Response is threshold-gated on baseline pain (>6 → 65.8%, rising to 85.7% at >8), and the strongest predictor was neurotoxic-chemo exposure (multivariate OR 5.1). The RT decision this moves is timing: refer for celiac SRS before neurotoxic agents. SRS dose/fractionation not reported in source (parent Lancet Oncol trial).
studies.monday_clinic: In pancreatic-cancer pts with severe retroperitoneal pain and no prior neurotoxic chemo (SPIN 2), this supports earlier celiac plexus SRS, where 89% (n=19) achieved pain response; it does not extend to SPIN-0 pts, whose response was 32% (n=31).

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Post-hoc predictor analysis of the pivotal **single-arm phase 2** celiac plexus SRS trial (NCT03323489, Lancet Oncol 2024). **90 evaluable pts**; univariate/multivariate logistic regression with 500-iteration bootstrap internal validation.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Pancreatic-cancer pts with intractable retroperitoneal (celiac) cancer pain treated on the phase 2 SRS trial. Age and BMI were the prior-reported predictors; median baseline values not in source.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Celiac plexus radiosurgery, target **D12-L2**. **Dose and fractionation not reported in source** (specified in the parent Lancet Oncol trial), so transferability of the technique can't be judged here.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Pain response = **≥2-point reduction** in average BPI-SF pain from baseline to **3 weeks**. Score performance judged by optimism-corrected AUC.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Two independent predictors (severe baseline pain, no prior neurotoxic chemo) build a 0-2 score with a monotonic response gradient (see tables). Discrimination modest, **AUC 0.714** corrected.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Celiac plexus SRS is a novel non-invasive palliative option **recently added to NCCN guidelines**. This analysis converts the parent trial's flat response into an actionable selection score and argues for earlier use, before neurotoxic chemotherapy.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents pancreatic-cancer pts with intractable retroperitoneal pain evaluated for celiac plexus SRS. Does not represent pts already exposed to neurotoxic chemotherapy (lower predicted response) or non-pancreatic retroperitoneal pain.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Post-hoc, single-arm data; predictors are associative. Internal bootstrap validation only (optimism-corrected **AUC 0.714**), external validation required. Subjective 3-week pain endpoint; score not prospectively tested.

open_questions: External validation of the SPIN score in an independent cohort
Whether earlier SRS before neurotoxic chemo improves outcomes prospectively
Durability of pain response beyond 3 weeks

Lay Summary
• In women having surgery to remove the breast for cancer treatment or prevention, the
optimal positioning of an implant (above the chest muscle, or below it) to rebuild the breast is
currently unclear.
• In the international PREPEC trial that was designed together with patient advocates, women
were assigned by chance (i.e., randomized) to receive the implant above or below the
muscle.
• The primary endpoint of the study was long-term patient-reported physical well-being of the
chest.
• Rebuilding the breast with an implant above the chest muscle resulted in better physical
well-being of the chest after two years (difference of 4.8 points on the BREAST-Q scale).
• However, implants placed above the chest muscle were more likely to undergo unexpected
removal or replacement (21% versus 15% of patients).

Perioperative MIBC: An Evolving Standard
Standard of care is defined by cisplatin-eligibility - with immunotherapy-based "sandwich" regimens
reshaping practice.
Cisplatin-ineligible or declining
• KEYNOTE-905: perioperative enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab (EVP) is now standard
• 3 cycles EVP → cystectomy → 6 cycles EV + 14 cycles P:
• NEW: VOLGA: preoperative EV + perioperative durvalumab + tremelimumab
• EV + durva improved EFS & OS vs cystectomy * approved adjuvant therapy; EV + durva/treme improved EFS (press release)?
Cisplatin-eligible
• NIAGARA: pre-op Gem/Cis + perioperative durvalumab (24-mo OS 82.2% vs 75.2% with Gem/Cis alone)
• VESPER: established ddMVAC > Gem/Cis on survival (5-yr OS 66% vs 57%) but greater toxicity*
• NEW: KEYNOTE-B15: perioperative EVP improved OS vs Gem/Cis (under FDA review)

Management Post-pCR: Continue or Stop?
For the sandwich regimens, standard practice continues planned
adjuvant therapy regardless of pathologic response.
Standard practice today
• NIAGARA regimen: Resume durvalumab for another 8 months post-cystectomy:
Perioperative EVP: Resume EVP post-cystectomy
Notably, not all patients in perioperative trials completed adjuvant therapy, sometimes due to toxicity
Cisplatin-based chemo alone: Surveillance after pCR is standard
Unanswered questions
What is the relative contribution of the pre- vs postoperative components?
Can adjuvant therapy be safely de-escalated based on pCR or other biomarkers?

Conclusions
• This single-arm study of amivantamab + lazertinib (n=49) in 1L atypical EGFR-mutated advanced NSCLC
demonstrates a clinically meaningful median OS of ~3.5 years
• Responses were durable regardless of demographics, baseline tumor mutations, and disease characteristic:
• With longer follow-up, the safety profile of IV amiantamab + lazertinib was consistent with prior reports1,2,
with no new safety signals
Amivantamab + lazertinib as a 1L treatment has now shown durable survival
in both common and atypical EGFR-mutated advanced NSCLC

(Neo-CRAG): a randomized, multicenter, phase 3 trial.
Rui-Hua Xu, Wei Wang, Yu-Jing Zhang, Zhiwei Zhou, et al. Sun Yat-sen University Cancer Center & 13 centers in China
44**
Background
• Neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy (CRT) has
been proposed to improve outcomes over
neoadjuvant chemotherapy (CT) in patients
with locally advanced gastric cancer (LAGC).
• Neo-CRAG is the first phase 3 trial to confirm
its efficacy and safety in high-risk LAGC
(2cT3N2) with standardized D2 resection.
Perioperative chemoradiotherapy is an effective strategy for high-
risk LAGC cases requiring enhanced locoregional control.

448 patients underwent D2 gastrectomy. pCR

the CRT group (20/213, 9.4%), compared with
the CT group (38/208, 18.3%).
Grade 3+ hematologic toxicity was relatively
higher in the CRT group (44/302 [14.6%] VS
31/301 [10.3%]).
Grade 3+ postoperative complication rates
were comparable 20/223[9.0% VS 17/225

The SPIN Score, a simple clinical score to predict pain response following celiac plexus
Abstract # 546246
Board # 194
radiosurgery for retroperitoneal cancer pain in pancreatic cancer.
Yaacov Lawrence Lawrence, Marcin Miszczyk, Dayssy Alexandra Diaz Pardo, Artur Aguiar, Dror Limon, M. Raphael Pfeffer, Michael Buckstein, Aisling S Barry, Adam P. Dicker, Camilla Zimmermann,
Jordan Kharofa, Ofer Margalit, Orly Yariv, Ofir Morag, David Hausner, Maoz Ben-Ayun, Talia Golan, Laura A. Dawson, Zvi Symon
SHEBA
Tel HaShomer
The Benjamin (Beni) Davidai
Department of
Radiation Oncology
Background
Celiac plexus radiosurgery is a novel non-invasive palliative treatment that was
recently added to NCCN guidelines. In the pivotal phase 2 trial (NCT03323489;
Lancet Oncol 2024;25:1070-79), pain response was 53% (95% Cl 42-64%).
Prespecified exploratory analysis identified age and BMI as predictors; however, no
clinically actionable patient selection tool was developed. We sought to identify
additional predictors and create a practical risk stratification score.
Methods
Post-hoc analysis of 90 evaluable patients from the phase 2 trial. Pain response was
defined per protocol (22-point reduction in average pain from baseline to three
weeks, on Brief Pain Inventory-Short Form). Candidate baseline predictors were
examined by univariate and multivariate logistic regression. Only variables
remaining significant in multivariate analysis were included in the final score to
ensure independence and avoid overfitting. Internal validation used bootstrap
resampling (500 iterations with replacement) to calculate optimism-corrected AUC.
Analysis performed using Stata IC/16.1.
Results
Univariate analysis identified 4 significant predictors: neurotoxic chemotherapy
exposure (OR 5.33, 95% Cl 2.13-13.4, p<0.001), baseline pain intensity (OR 1.73,
p=0.003), age (OR 1.06, p=0.014), and therapy line (OR 0.65, p=0.04). On
multivariate analysis, only neurotoxic exposure (OR 5.1, p=0.009) and baseline pain
(OR 1.8, p=0.003) remained independently significant predictors. Age and therapy
line lost significance due to collinearity.
Dose-response analysis
confirmed
pain threshold
optimization: >5
(61.5%
response), >6 (65.8%), >7 (83.3%), >8 (85.7%), with >6 providing optimal
discrimination.
We created the SPIN (Severe Pain + Intact Nerves) score (0-2
points): baseline Pain >6 (+1), No prior Neurotoxic chemotherapy (+1). Response
rates by score: 0 points: 32% (n=31); 1 point: 53% (n=40); 2 points: 89% (n=19). The
2-variable model achieved an apparent AUC=0.716. Following bootstrapping, the
optimism-corrected AUC was= 0.714
Conclusions
• The simple score allows identification of distinct patient subgroups with markedly different probabilities of pain response
following celiac plexus radiosurgery.
• This suggests the intervention should be considered earlier in the disease course, before exposure to neurotoxic agents.
External validation is needed prior to clinical implementation.
